criteria: The agent has read Section 2.1 completely to understand the generative attack framework: generator G_θ maps benign input to adversarial candidate, projector P enforces L∞-budget, and generator is supervised by a white-box surrogate model.

The agent has read Section 2.2 completely to understand the semantic variability metric: cross-block standard deviation of foreground IoU between k-means cluster-derived activation masks and foreground masks across early, mid, and late generator blocks.
The agent has read Section 3 completely to understand the two key components of SCGA: Mean Teacher-based EMA feature smoothing and the self-feature consistency loss applied to early generator blocks.

The agent has read Algorithm 1 to understand the full SCGA training loop: sample mini-batch, acquire student and teacher intermediate features, generate and project adversarial examples, compute combined loss, update student via backprop, then EMA-update teacher.

The agent has read Section 4.1 to understand the three limitations (L1: single metric reporting, L2: arbitrary data subsets, L3: narrow victim set) motivating the introduction of ACR and expanded evaluation.
The agent has read Section 4.2 completely to understand the formal definitions of all four evaluation metrics: Acc., ASR, FR, and the novel ACR (Accidental Correction Rate), as given in Equation 6.

The agent has read Table 2 to understand the cross-model transferability evaluation setup: 21 victim architectures spanning CNN, Transformer, Mixer, and Mamba families, with results reported as delta percentages relative to each baseline.

The agent has read Table 3 to understand the cross-domain/task evaluation setup: three fine-grained domains (CUB-200-2011, Stanford Cars, FGVC Aircraft) and two cross-task settings (semantic segmentation with mIoU, object detection with mAP50).

The agent has read Table 5 to understand both ablation studies: (a) comparing early, mid, late, and all-block targets for the consistency loss, and (b) comparing subsets of components {L_adv, MT, L_cons, PT} in the cross-task/domain/model settings.

The agent has read Table 7 to understand the hyperparameter sensitivity for λ_cons ∈ {0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7, 0.9} and η ∈ {0.9, 0.95, 0.98, 0.99, 0.995}, and the early-block selection ablation comparing block 1 alone, block 2 alone, and both blocks 1 & 2.

The plan includes implementation of a student-teacher generator pair sharing the same ResNet-based architecture, with the teacher initialized from the student weights and updated only via EMA (no gradient updates to teacher).
The plan includes the EMA teacher parameter update rule: θ'_t ← η * θ'_{t-1} + (1-η) * θ_t, with η as the smoothing coefficient hyperparameter.

The plan includes the self-feature consistency loss as a hinge-based loss summed over early blocks L_early, using cosine similarity between student features g_s^ℓ and teacher features g_t^ℓ, with learnable block-wise weights W_cons.
The plan includes the adversarial loss as the cosine similarity between surrogate features of benign and adversarial images at layer k: L_adv = cos(F_k(x), F_k(x_adv)).
The plan includes the combined final loss: L = L_adv + λ_cons * L_cons, with λ_cons as the weight for the consistency loss.
The plan includes extracting intermediate feature activations from the first two residual blocks of the generator (early blocks) for both student and teacher to compute the consistency loss.
The plan includes implementing ACR as the proportion of initially misclassified samples (set I where f(x) ≠ y) for which the adversarial example causes a correct prediction (f(x+δ) = y).
The plan includes implementation of all six baseline attacks: CDA, LTP, BIA, GAMA, FACL, and PDCL, with SCGA integrated into each.
The plan includes cross-model evaluation on the full ImageNet-1K validation set across 21 victim architectures covering CNN, Vision Transformer, MLP-Mixer, and Mamba model families.
The plan includes cross-domain evaluation on CUB-200-2011, Stanford Cars, and FGVC Aircraft datasets using the four classification metrics (Acc., ASR, FR, ACR).
The plan includes cross-task evaluation using semantic segmentation with DeepLabV3+ and SegFormer (mIoU metric) and object detection with Faster R-CNN and DETR (mAP50 metric).
The plan includes evaluation against robust defenses: adversarially trained models (AT IncV3, AT ViT, AT ConvNeXt) and input pre-processing methods (JPEG, BDR, R&P).
The plan includes ablation studies testing the consistency loss applied to early, mid, late, and all generator blocks, and component ablations with subsets of {L_adv, MT, L_cons, PT}.
The plan includes hyperparameter sensitivity experiments sweeping λ_cons over {0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7, 0.9} and η over {0.9, 0.95, 0.98, 0.99, 0.995}, with default settings λ_cons=0.7 and η=0.999.

The plan includes semantic variability analysis using k-means clustering on generator feature maps to separate foreground and background, computing foreground IoU, and reporting its cross-block standard deviation.
The code for the EMA teacher parameter update has been implemented as Equation 2: θ'_t ← η * θ'_{t-1} + (1-η) * θ_t, applied per training step without gradient computation on the teacher.

The code for the student and teacher generator pair has been implemented, where the teacher shares the same architecture as the student and is initialized from the student's weights at the start of training.
The code for the self-feature consistency loss has been implemented as Equation 3: L_cons = Σ_{ℓ=1}^{L_early} W_cons · [τ - ⟨g_s^ℓ, g_t^ℓ⟩ / (‖g_s^ℓ‖_2 ‖g_t^ℓ‖_2)]_+, where [·]_+ = max(0, ·).
The code for the learnable block-wise loss weights W_cons has been implemented as a softmax over a learnable parameter vector of size |L|, applied per block in the consistency loss.

The code for the adversarial loss has been implemented as Equation 4: L_adv = cos(F_k(x), F_k(x_adv)), using cosine similarity between surrogate model features at layer k for benign and adversarial images.
The code for the combined final loss has been implemented as Equation 5: L = L_adv + λ_cons * L_cons, with λ_cons as a configurable hyperparameter defaulting to 0.7.

The code for the full SCGA training loop has been implemented following Algorithm 1: sample mini-batch, extract student and teacher early-block features, generate and project adversarial examples, compute combined loss, backpropagate to update student, then perform EMA update of teacher.
The code for the perturbation projector P(·) has been implemented, enforcing the L∞ perturbation budget such that ‖P(x̃_adv) - x‖_∞ ≤ ε.

The code for extracting intermediate features from the generator's early blocks (first two residual blocks) has been implemented for both the student generator (with gradients) and the teacher generator (without gradients).

The code for the ResNet-based perturbation generator has been implemented with six residual blocks grouped into three partitions: early (blocks 1-2, downsampling), mid (blocks 3-4, residual learning), and late (blocks 5-6, upsampling).
The code for the ACR metric has been implemented as Equation 6: ACR = |{x ∈ I | f(x) ≠ y ∧ f(x+δ) = y}| / |I|, where I is the set of initially misclassified samples.
The code for the ASR metric has been implemented as Equation 6: ASR = |{x ∈ C | f(x) = y ∧ f(x+δ) ≠ y}| / |C|, where C is the set of correctly classified benign samples.
The code for the FR and Acc. metrics has been implemented as Equation 6: FR = |{x ∈ D | f(x) ≠ f(x+δ)}| / |D| and Acc. = |{x ∈ D | f(x+δ) = y}| / |D|.
The code for the CDA baseline attack has been implemented and integrated with SCGA as a combined training objective.
The code for the LTP baseline attack has been implemented and integrated with SCGA as a combined training objective.
The code for the BIA baseline attack has been implemented and integrated with SCGA; BIA serves as the primary baseline whose feature similarity-based adversarial loss forms the backbone of SCGA.
The code for the GAMA baseline attack has been implemented and integrated with SCGA as a combined training objective.
The code for the FACL baseline attack has been implemented and integrated with SCGA as a combined training objective.
The code for the PDCL baseline attack has been implemented and integrated with SCGA as a combined training objective.
The code for loading and processing the ImageNet-1K training dataset (source domain D_src) has been implemented for training the generator.
The code for cross-model evaluation on the full ImageNet-1K validation set has been implemented, covering 21 victim architectures from CNN, Transformer, Mixer, and Mamba families.
The code for cross-domain evaluation has been implemented, loading and processing CUB-200-2011, Stanford Cars, and FGVC Aircraft datasets and computing Acc., ASR, FR, ACR for each.
The code for cross-task semantic segmentation evaluation has been implemented using DeepLabV3+ and SegFormer victim models with the mIoU metric.
The code for cross-task object detection evaluation has been implemented using Faster R-CNN and DETR victim models with the mAP50 metric.
The code for evaluation against adversarially trained models (AT IncV3, AT ViT, AT ConvNeXt) and input pre-processing defenses (JPEG, BDR, R&P) has been implemented.

The code for the k-means clustering-based semantic variability analysis has been implemented: cluster generator feature maps to separate foreground/background, compute foreground IoU with ground-truth masks, and report cross-block standard deviation.

The code for the generator block-level feature difference visualization has been implemented: apply cross-channel average pooling to activation tensors and threshold the resulting difference map between baseline and SCGA generators.

The code for spectral energy analysis has been implemented: decompose generator intermediate feature activations into low-frequency (LF) and high-frequency (HF) bands and compute normalized energy ratios for each generator block.

The code for the block-selection ablation has been implemented: apply self-feature consistency loss to early-only, mid-only, late-only, and all-blocks configurations, keeping all other components fixed.

The code for the component ablation has been implemented: train with all combinations of {L_adv, MT, L_cons} as well as a plain-teacher (PT) configuration (student-copy teacher without EMA, with and without L_cons).

The code for the hyperparameter sensitivity sweep has been implemented for λ_cons ∈ {0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7, 0.9} and η ∈ {0.9, 0.95, 0.98, 0.99, 0.995}, and for single-block vs. dual-block early selection.
The SCGA training has been executed successfully for all six baseline integrations (CDA, LTP, BIA, GAMA, FACL, PDCL) on the ImageNet-1K source domain without errors.
The cross-model evaluation code has been executed successfully, producing Acc., ASR, FR, and ACR delta results for all 21 victim architectures across all six baselines with and without SCGA.
The cross-domain and cross-task evaluation code has been executed successfully, producing results on CUB-200-2011, Stanford Cars, FGVC Aircraft, and SS/OD tasks for all baseline + SCGA combinations.
The defense evaluation code has been executed successfully, producing Acc., ASR, FR, and ACR for BIA baseline and BIA with SCGA against AT models and input pre-processing defenses.
The ablation study code has been executed successfully, producing cross-model, cross-domain, and cross-task results for all block-selection and component-ablation configurations.

The hyperparameter sensitivity experiments have been executed successfully, producing domain, model, SS, and OD accuracy results for all values of λ_cons and η, and for single vs. dual early-block selection.

The spectral energy analysis has been executed successfully, producing normalized low-frequency and high-frequency band energy ratios for early, mid, and late generator blocks for all six baseline comparisons.

The execution outcomes confirm that the semantic variability (std dev of foreground IoU) is reduced when SCGA is applied versus the baseline for most methods (e.g., CDA: 2.77 → 1.51, GAMA: 2.46 → 1.41, FACL: 2.19 → 1.17, PDCL: 2.08 → 0.71), as reported in Figure 1(c).

The execution outcomes confirm that early generator blocks consistently have higher foreground IoU than mid and late blocks across all baselines, with IoU degrading from early to late (e.g., CDA: 37.72 → 36.74 → 32.14), as shown in Figure 1(c).

The execution outcomes match the cross-model results in Table 2: SCGA with CDA yields the largest average ASR improvement (+8.55%p) and SCGA with LTP yields consistent improvement (+8.47%p avg.), while SCGA with PDCL shows minimal change (+0.09%p avg.).

The execution outcomes confirm the pattern in Table 2 that CNN-family architectures generally benefit more from SCGA than Transformer-family architectures, consistent with the paper's analysis of surrogate-objective interplay.

The execution outcomes match the cross-domain results in Table 3: SCGA with CDA shows large gains on FGVC Aircraft (Acc. −22.58%p, ASR +27.74%p, FR +26.44%p) and SCGA generally yields more pronounced improvements in cross-domain settings than cross-model.

The execution outcomes match the cross-task results in Table 3: SCGA integration with CDA and LTP baselines consistently reduces mIoU for semantic segmentation and mAP50 for object detection, confirming label-agnostic cross-task generalization.

The execution outcomes match Table 4: SCGA improves average ASR from 23.75% to 26.52% and average FR from 32.78% to 35.28% against defense mechanisms, while reducing ACR from 9.51% to 9.01% on average.

The execution outcomes match the block-selection ablation in Table 5(a): applying consistency loss to early blocks achieves lower Acc. and higher ASR/FR than applying it to mid, late, or all blocks in both cross-domain and cross-model settings.

The execution outcomes match the component ablation in Table 5(b): the full SCGA configuration (L_adv + MT + L_cons) achieves the best Acc. (lowest) and ASR/FR (highest) across cross-task, cross-domain, and cross-model settings; the plain teacher (PT) configuration underperforms MT.

The execution outcomes confirm that λ_cons=0.7 and η=0.999 provide the best overall balance across cross-domain, cross-model, SS, and OD settings, as reported in Table 7, and that using both early blocks (1 & 2) outperforms using only block 1 or only block 2.

The execution outcomes match the spectral energy results in Table 6: early-block anchoring raises low-frequency energy and suppresses high-frequency energy for most baselines (e.g., CDA early: LF 0.82 → 0.91, HF 0.18 → 0.09), with the effect propagating to mid and late blocks.
